original_abstract: The efficient activation and hydrogenation of CO2 into methanol represent a critical pathway for the utilization of captured carbon emissions. In this study, we explore the catalytic performance of size-selected Cu4 clusters supported on amorphous Al2O3 thin films. By employing in situ grazing incidence X-ray absorption spectroscopy, we monitored the oxidation state of these subnanometer clusters under near-atmospheric reaction conditions. Our results demonstrate that Cu4 clusters are exceptionally active for CH3OH synthesis, outperforming previously reported low-pressure catalysts even at low CO2 partial pressures. Density functional theory (DFT) calculations complement these findings, revealing that the unique coordination environment of the Cu4 cluster significantly lowers the activation barriers for the CO2-to-methanol pathway compared to bulk copper surfaces or larger nanoparticles. These findings suggest that precision-engineered, small copper clusters offer a highly efficient and promising route for the sustainable recycling of CO2 into value-added chemicals.
humanized_abstract: The activation and hydrogenation of CO2 into methanol is one of the most desirable and necessary methods to utilize the captured carbon dioxide. Here, we investigate the catalytic performance of size-selected Cu4 clusters supported on amorphous Al2O3 thin film. In situ grazing incidence X-ray absorption near edge structure (XANES) measurements were carried out to understand the oxidation states of these subnanometer clusters under the near-atmospheric condition. Interestingly, Cu4 clusters demonstrated outstanding activities toward the formation of CH3OH under near-atmospheric condition even at low CO2 concentrations which is even better than the previous reported low pressure catalysts. By combining the density functional theory calculations, the novel coordination sites provided by the Cu4 clusters were found to be responsible for the increased activities. Our research provides the first in situ investigation on the catalytic activities of small Cu clusters towards the direct CO2 to CH3OH conversion, which demonstrates that precision engineering of small copper clusters to obtain improved catalytic performance for the effective utilization of CO2 in the synthesis of valuable chemicals.
